content:
In the following differential equation, the numerically obtained value of $$y(t)$$, at $$t=1$$ is ___________ (Round off to 2 decimal places).

$${{dy} \over {dt}} = {{{e^{ - \alpha t}}} \over {2 + \alpha t}},\alpha = 0.01$$ and $$y(0) = 0$$
explanation:
Given : $\frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{e^{-\alpha t}}{2+\alpha t}$ $\alpha=0.01$ ..........(i)

$y(0)=0$ (initial)

$$ f(t, y)=\frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{e^{-0.01 t}}{2+0.01 t} $$

Euler's method : Step size $h=t$ (given)

$$ \begin{aligned} y_1 & =y_0+h f(t, y) \quad\left(\text { at } t_0=0, y_0=0\right) \\\\ & =0+1\left[\frac{e^{-0.01 \times 0}}{2+0.01 \times 0}\right] \\\\ & =0+1 \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{2}=0.5 \end{aligned} $$

So, at $t=1, \quad y_1=0.5$

The solution of the first order differential equation $$\mathop x\limits^ \bullet  \left( t \right) =  - 3\,x\left( t \right),\,x\left( 0 \right) = {x_0}\,\,\,\,$$ is
If at every point of a certain curve , the slope of the tangent equals $${{ - 2x} \over y},$$  the curve is _______.

Consider ordinary differential equations given by $\dot{x}_1(t)=2 x_2(t), \dot{x}_2(t)=r(t)$ with initial conditions $x_1(0)=1$ and $x_2(0)=0$. If $r(t)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t<0\end{array}\right.$, then $t=1, x_1(t)=$ _____________ (Round off to the nearest integer).
explanation:
$$ \left[\begin{array}{l} \dot{x}_1(t) \\ \dot{x}_2(t) \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x_1(t) \\ x_2(t) \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{l} 0 \\ 1 \end{array}\right] r(t) $$

$$ \text { From question, } \left.\quad r(t)=u(t) \quad \begin{array}{l} x_1(0)=1 \\ x_2(0)=0 \end{array}\right\} \text { Given } $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} A & =\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ {[s I-A] } & =\left[\begin{array}{cc} s & -2 \\ 0 & s \end{array}\right] \\ {[s I-A]^{-1} } & =\frac{1}{s^2}\left[\begin{array}{ll} s & 2 \\ 0 & s \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{s} & \frac{2}{s^2} \\ 0 & \frac{1}{s} \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \text { State transition matrix, } \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \phi(t)=L^{-1}\left\{[s I-A]^{-1}\right\}=L^{-1}\left\{\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{s} & \frac{2}{s^2} \\ 0 & \frac{1}{s} \end{array}\right]\right\} \\ & \left.\phi(t)=\left[\begin{array}{cc} u(t) & 2 t u(t) \\ 0 & u(t) \end{array}\right]\right\} \\ \therefore & x(t)=\phi(t) \cdot x(0)+L^{-1}\left\{[s I-A]^{-1} B \cdot U(s)\right\}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Here, }[S I-A]^{-1} B \cdot U(s)=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{s} & \frac{2}{s^2} \\ 0 & \frac{1}{s} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 0 \\ 1 \end{array}\right] \frac{1}{s}=\left[\begin{array}{c} \frac{2}{s^2} \\ \frac{1}{s} \end{array}\right] \frac{1}{s}=\left[\begin{array}{c} \frac{2}{s^3} \\ \frac{1}{s^2} \end{array}\right] \\ & \therefore \quad L^{-1}\left\{[s I-A]^{-1} B U(s)\right\}=L^{-1}\left\{\left[\begin{array}{c} \frac{2}{s^3} \\ \frac{1}{s^2} \end{array}\right]\right\}=\left[\begin{array}{c} t^2 U(t) \\ t u(t) \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} t^2 \\ t \end{array}\right] U(t) \\ & \therefore \quad x(t)=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 t \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] U(t) \cdot\left[\begin{array}{c} x_1(0) \\ x_2(0) \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{c} t^2 \\ t \end{array}\right] U(t) \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & =\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 t \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right] u(t)+\left[\begin{array}{c} t^2 \\ t \end{array}\right] u(t) \\ x(t) & =\left[\begin{array}{c} t^2+1 \\ t \end{array}\right] u(t)=\left[\begin{array}{l} x_1(t) \\ x_2(t) \end{array}\right] \\ x_1(t) & =\left(t^2+1\right) u(t) \end{aligned}\\ &\text { At } t=1 \text {, }\\ &x_1(t)=\left(1^2+1\right) u(1)=2 \end{aligned} $$

content: Which of the following differential equations is/are nonlinear?
explanation:
To determine which differential equations are nonlinear, we need to analyze whether the equations involve nonlinear functions of the dependent variable $ x(t) $ or its derivatives. A differential equation is nonlinear if it includes nonlinear terms, such as products or compositions of $ x(t) $ and its derivatives.

Let's break down each option:

Option A:

$$ t \, x(t) + \frac{dx(t)}{dt} = t^2 e^t $$

In this equation, $ t \, x(t) $ is a product of $ t $ (which is an independent variable) and $ x(t) $ (which is the dependent variable), but the term itself is linear in $ x(t) $. Also, $ \frac{dx(t)}{dt} $ appears linearly. Therefore, this is a linear differential equation.

Option B:

$$ \frac{1}{2} e^t + x(t) \frac{dx(t)}{dt} = 0 $$

Here, $ x(t) \frac{dx(t)}{dt} $ is a product of $ x(t) $ and its derivative $ \frac{dx(t)}{dt} $, which makes this term nonlinear. Hence, this is a nonlinear differential equation.

Option C:

$$ x(t) \cos t - \frac{dx(t)}{dt} \sin t = 1 $$

In this equation, $ x(t) \cos t $ is a product of a function of $ t $ and $ x(t) $, but it is linear in $ x(t) $. The term $ \frac{dx(t)}{dt} $, multiplied by $ \sin t $, remains linear in $ \frac{dx(t)}{dt} $. Thus, this is a linear differential equation.

Option D:

$$ x(t) + e^{\left(\frac{dx(t)}{dt}\right)} = 1 $$

In this equation, $ e^{\left(\frac{dx(t)}{dt}\right)} $ is an exponential function of the derivative $ \frac{dx(t)}{dt} $, which introduces nonlinearity. Hence, this is a nonlinear differential equation.

Based on the analysis, the nonlinear differential equations are:

Option B: $$ \frac{1}{2} e^t + x(t) \frac{dx(t)}{dt} = 0 $$

Option D: $$ x(t) + e^{\left(\frac{dx(t)}{dt}\right)} = 1 $$

Thus, the correct options are B and D.
